front: Religion: this Mahayana Buddhist tradition, founded by Bodhidharma, is called Chan in China, Seon in Korea, and Thien in Vietnam
Religion: seeks satori or kensho, a sudden insight into one's own Buddha-nature
Religion: its name derives from the Sanskrit word dhyana ("meditation"), transmitted via the Chinese term Chan
Religion: early history saw a split into Northern and Southern schools, disagreeing over "gradual" versus "sudden" enlightenment teachings
Religion: its three main surviving Japanese schools are Soto, Rinzai, and Obaku
Religion: legend holds it began when the Buddha silently held up a flower before his disciples, and only Mahakasyapa understood and smiled
Religion: its ink-wash painters, including Sesshu Toyo (who completed a 50-foot painting of the four seasons using "broken" and "splashed" styles) and Shubun, introduced the genre to Japan
Religion: its artists invented a portrait genre called sensho and a rock-garden landscaping style formed by raking patterns
Religion: painter Brice Marden used the subtitle "Early State" for several works in a series named for this tradition
Religion: 20th-century Kyoto School philosopher Keiji Nishitani synthesized existentialism with this tradition and its concept of mu ("nothingness")
Religion: Miyamoto Musashi's The Book of Five Rings (especially its "Book of Void") draws on this tradition's philosophy, later analyzed in Eugen Herrigel's Zen and the Art of Archery
Religion: Philip Kapleau founded a study center in Rochester, NY and wrote a book about this tradition's "three pillars"
Religion: Alan Watts wrote popular books about the "way" and "spirit" of this tradition
Religion: San Francisco Zen Center teacher Richard Baker resigned after an affair was revealed, in one of several Western sex scandals it faced in the 1980s
Religion: popularized in Germany by the psychotherapist Karlfried Graf Durckheim, who developed "initiation therapy"
Religion: Gestalt therapy, developed by Fritz and Laura Perls and taught at the Esalen Institute, blended Western therapeutic ideas with this tradition's meditative practices
Religion: Jack Kerouac's novel The Dharma Bums follows characters seeking enlightenment through this tradition, including the character Japhy Ryder, based on the poet Gary Snyder
Figure: cut off his own eyelids to avoid falling asleep during years of wall-facing meditation at the Shaolin Temple; in some tellings the eyelids grew into the first tea plants
Figure: his successor proved devotion by cutting off his own arm (or, in another telling, merely bowing and sitting in silence), earning the praise "you have attained my marrow"
Figure: traditionally credited as author of the Treatise on the Two Entrances and Four Practices
Figure: apocryphally taught the monks of the Shaolin Temple 18 forms that became the basis of kung fu
Figure: is the subject of several entries in the koan collection The Gateless Gate
Figure: after his death, followers reportedly found only a single shoe left in his grave
Figure: when he gave an enigmatic answer during a visit to Emperor Wu, an official claimed the bodhisattva Guanyin was speaking through him
Figure: won a legendary poetry contest against the monk Shenxiu by rejecting his comparison of the mind to a mirror needing constant polishing
Figure: traditionally the Sixth Patriarch of this tradition, said to have achieved sudden enlightenment upon hearing the Diamond Sutra recited
Figure: traditionally credited as the source of a sutra relating the dharma's transmission from his own teacher Hongren onward to his disciples
Text: unusual among sutras for its (apocryphal) autobiographical elements, relating the dharma's transmission from Hongren to Huineng and then to Huineng's own disciples
Text: named for the ordination "Platform" associated with the Sixth Patriarch Huineng
Practice: when a monk asked the teacher Zhaozhou whether a dog has Buddha-nature, he replied simply "Mu"
Practice: Hakuin Ekaku's koan asks what sound is made by one hand clapping
Practice: one koan asks the student to reveal his "original face" — the one he had before his parents were born
Practice: the saying "if you meet the Buddha, kill him" is among these teaching riddles, attributed to the master Linji
Practice: the huatou method of focusing on a single word from one of these riddles was introduced by the monk Dahui Zonggao
Text: compiled with commentary by the monk Wumen Huikai; its paradoxical title refers to a gate one must pass through, despite there being no actual gate, to reach enlightenment
Text: is the source of the "original face" koan
Text: compiles paradoxical koans of the Song Dynasty, including entries titled "The Highest Meaning of the Holy Truths" and "Hsueh Fang's Grain of Rice"
Text: frequently named alongside The Gateless Gate as one of the tradition's two major koan collections

Text: a set of ten pictures traditionally attributed to the monk Kakuan, depicting a herder tracking, catching, and riding an ox that eventually disappears (the eighth picture is blank, symbolizing transcendence)
Text: John Cage made a series of ten circular drawings imitating this work's imagery of catching and taming a bull
Denomination: revived in the 18th century by Hakuin Ekaku, who believed serving others was Zen's central concern; historically popular among the samurai; descends from the Chinese Linji school
Denomination: its patriarch, called Rinzai in Japan, explained "mystery in essence," "mystery in words," and "mystery in mystery" as the Three Gates
Denomination: emphasizes kensho, insight into one's Buddha-nature, attained through meditation on koans
Figure: founded the Soto school, the largest Zen sect in Japan; wrote The True Dharma Eye and promoted shikantaza, a silent "just sitting" meditation without active koan contemplation
Figure: taught that all living things, including plants, possess Buddha-nature
Figure: wrote the Bendowa, promoting the practice of zazen
Figure: the scholar most responsible for popularizing this tradition in the West; compared its concepts of satori and sunyata to the Christian mystic Meister Eckhart
Figure: wrote the essay "Zen and the Samurai" in Zen and Japanese Culture and a 1934 Introduction to Zen Buddhism
Figure: engaged in an interfaith dialogue with the Trappist monk Thomas Merton, published as the essay "Wisdom in Emptiness"
Figure: his lectures on meditation inspired John Cage's engagement with this tradition, which in turn led to Nam June Paik's video work "Zen for Film"
Figure: wrote the autobiography The Seven Storey Mountain; studied Zen in dialogues with D.T. Suzuki and the Confucian-trained Catholic convert John C.H. Wu
Figure: discussed Christ's kenosis (Philippians 2:7) in relation to Zen in his book Zen and the Birds of Appetite
Figure: helped establish the Engaged Buddhism movement alongside B.R. Ambedkar; founded the Plum Village Monastery in France after being banished from his home country
Figure: his teachings were adapted by Jon Kabat-Zinn in developing Mindfulness-Based Stress Reduction (MBSR)
